rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical multicomponent reaction for C-H tetrazolation of alkyl arenes and vicinal azidotetrazolation of alkenes using acetonitrile and TMSN3
electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation: for alkyl arenes, anode oxidizes the arene to a radical cation that loses a proton to form a benzyl radical, which is further oxidized to a benzyl cation; for alkenes, anode oxidizes azide (from TMSN3) to azido radical which adds to alkene, then anodic oxidation gives β-azido carbocation. Cathode reduces protons to H2.

key_intermediates.name: ethylbenzene radical cation (phenyl radical cation intermediate)

electron_flow: For alkyl arenes: anode oxidizes the arene (SET) to a radical cation, deprotonation gives a benzyl radical, a second anodic SET forms the benzyl cation, MeCN traps it as a Ritter nitrilium ion, and azide closes the ring to give the tetrazole; protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode. For alkenes: anodic oxidation of TMSN3-derived azide gives N3•, which adds to the alkene, the resulting C-radical is further oxidized to a β-azido cation, trapped by MeCN/azide to form the vicinal azidotetrazole.
rate_determining_step: Anodic single-electron oxidation of the alkyl arene (transition state bears positive charge build-up, ρ = -2.97)

probe_reagents.name: (1-cyclopropyl-1-phenyl)ethylene (radical clock 54)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened azidotetrazole 55 obtained (21%, 7.5:1), confirming involvement of an azido radical that adds to the alkene

probe_reagents.name: p-substituted ethylbenzenes (Hammett series)
probe_reagents.observation: Hammett ρ = -2.97 (vs σp+), much more negative than typical HAT (ρ > -1), indicating positive charge build-up in TS, consistent with radical cation/SET mechanism rather than HAT

probe_reagents.name: selectivity probe 21' (two benzylic C-H of different BDE: 88 vs 83.3 kcal/mol)
probe_reagents.observation: Sites of similar BDE but different electronic properties were well distinguished, disproving HAT pathway

probe_reagents.name: benzyltrimethylsilane (56)
probe_reagents.observation: Gave tetrazole 7 (34%) exclusively via C-Si cleavage of the radical cation, supporting radical cation (not HAT) mechanism

probe_reagents.name: omission of TMSN3 (control)
probe_reagents.observation: Ritter-type acetamide product 57 isolated in 27%, confirming benzyl carbocation/nitrilium intermediate

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of substrates and TMSN3
probe_reagents.observation: Ep/2 (ethylbenzene) = 2.43 V; with HFIP drops to 2.01 V; TMSN3 Ep/2 = 0.92 V; styrene Ep/2 = 2.21 V — for alkene case TMSN3 is oxidized preferentially, generating N3•

notes: Authors explicitly disfavor a HAT pathway (via electrogenerated azido radical abstracting benzylic C-H) based on Hammett ρ = -2.97, BDE-insensitive selectivity in 21', and selective C-Si cleavage of benzyl-TMS 56 — favoring instead stepwise ET/PT (radical cation → deprotonation → benzyl radical → second SET → cation). For the alkene azidotetrazolation, an alternative diazide pathway (III) via radical-radical coupling (path b) or polar TMSN3 trapping of cation (path c) is considered disfavored because excess MeCN outcompetes low-concentration TMSN3 at the anode. HFIP is crucial as a radical-cation-stabilizing co-solvent that lowers the substrate oxidation potential.
